entries.title: South wind

notes.value: Book designed by John S. Fass; printed by the Stratford Press; issued in a slipcase.

description.value: Explores the pleasures of the hedonistic life. Set on the fictional island of Nepenthe (another depiction of Capri), South Wind tells the very loosely developed story of Thomas Heard, Bishop of Bambopo, during the season of the sirocco (south wind), which considerably affects the attitudes and behavior of the islanders. He encounters people holding various unorthodox views on moral and sexual questions.

contributions: Fass, John S. (John Stroble), 1890-1973, book designer

entries.title: South wind
South wind
entries.by_statement: by Norman Douglas, author of Old Calabria

notes.value:
"One hundred and fifty large-paper copies signed by the author."

Printed on blue linen-finish paper.

description.value: [This novel] is set on an imaginary island called Nepenthe, located off the coast of Italy in the Tyrrhenian Sea, a thinly fictionalized description of Capri's residents and visitors. The novel's discussion of moral and sexual issues caused considerable debate. --Wikipedia.com.

notes.value:
"Of this edition of South wind, written by Norman Douglas, with a special introduction by Carl Van Doren, illustrated by Carlotta Petrina, designed by Elmer Adler and printed by the Pynson Printers in New York, fifteen hundred copies completed ... for distribution to the members of the Limited Editions Club ... Signed by Carlotta Petrina"--Colophon.
Issued in a slipcase.

Actually the second impression of the second English ed., with inttroduction.

Woolf A19d.

Originally published: New York : Dodd, Mead, 1928.
Edition of 1982 published without illustrations.
King copy donated from the estates of John Weigel and Milton White

notes.value: Burns Library copy: Author's presentation copy to Graham Greene reads "who is going to be plagued to death by the damned thing," dated from Capri, 1 Feb. 1949 ; autograph: Graham Greene ; copiously annotated text in Greene's hand ; extensive notes in Greene's hand at front and back of text.
